chunks.text: The 2-3 December 1984 Bhopal methyl isocyanate (MIC) release at Union Carbide India Ltd (~3,800 immediate deaths, ~15,000+ subsequent, ~500,000 exposed) remains the deadliest industrial chemical disaster and the canonical reference for community-scale toxic release MCI. Operational features and lessons: (1) MIC is a highly volatile, water-reactive isocyanate — clinical signs include eye irritation/blindness, severe pulmonary edema (delayed up to hours post-exposure), and chemical burns to upper and lower airway; treatment is supportive with high-flow oxygen, bronchodilators, corticosteroids, fluid management for non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema; (2) shelter-in-place vs evacuation decision is plume-direction-dependent — at Bhopal, residents who fled into the gas plume in panic suffered higher mortality than those who covered face with wet cloth and remained low (gas heavier than air); modern doctrine pre-distributes plume modeling to community evacuation officers; (3) downwind hospital surge: Bhopal hospitals received >50,000 in 24 hours, with no antidote stockpile and no chemical PPE — modern petrochemical zones require pre-positioned antidote caches (atropine, pralidoxime, hydroxocobalamin per chemistry) and Level B PPE for ED staff; (4) long-term morbidity: pulmonary fibrosis, blindness, ocular surface damage, neuropsychiatric sequelae; cohort registries persist 40 years post-incident. EmergencyNet implication: ZONE_CHEM polygons must integrate live wind-direction modeling, not static circles.

The 26 April 1986 Chernobyl Reactor 4 explosion + graphite fire (~30 acute radiation deaths, ~134 acute radiation syndrome cases, long-term thyroid cancer cluster, evacuation of ~350,000) defined modern radiological emergency response in the era before robotic scene access. Operational lessons relevant to civilian response: (1) acute radiation syndrome (ARS) dose response: <1 Gy mostly subclinical, 1-2 Gy GI prodrome (nausea, vomiting at 2-6h post-exposure), 2-6 Gy bone marrow syndrome (neutropenia 7-14 days, infection mortality without supportive care), >6 Gy GI/CNS syndrome (mostly fatal); time-to-vomiting is the field triage proxy for dose; (2) firefighters in the first wave received 4-16 Gy doses without dosimetry awareness — modern doctrine mandates real-time dosimeter alarms, time-and-distance discipline (ALARA), shift rotation by cumulative dose, not by time; (3) evacuation iodine: KI tablets distributed in Pripyat 36 hours post-release, retrospectively identified as too late for some thyroid I-131 uptake protection; modern: pre-distributed KI in 50 km radius of nuclear facilities, with administration triggered by plume model + measurement, not just news; (4) cleanup ('liquidator') worker doctrine: rotation by accumulated dose; lifetime limit per worker; pension and medical follow-up for life. Relevance: EmergencyNet anomaly_detector with febrile + GI + cytopenia cluster = consider radiation scenario.

Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) fires represent an emerging high-risk industrial hazard as grid-scale lithium installations (>100 MWh) proliferate. Reference incidents: 2019 Surprise AZ McMicken BESS fire (4 firefighters injured by deflagration when opening enclosure), 2022 Moss Landing CA fire (largest BESS fire to date, weeks of off-gassing), 2024 multiple Chinese and EU BESS facility fires. Operational features: (1) thermal runaway in lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP, dominant chemistry post-2022) is slower than NMC but produces hydrogen fluoride (HF), hydrogen cyanide (HCN), and carbon monoxide off-gas — forming flammable + toxic mixture inside enclosure that detonates on fresh air entry; SOPs now: do not open enclosure without ventilation cool-down, sometimes 24-72 hours; (2) water suppression appropriate (LFP non-water-reactive, unlike sodium or magnesium batteries) but generates contaminated runoff requiring containment; bermed retention or absorbent berms standard; (3) PPE: SCBA + Level B for proximate operations (HF + HCN); approach distance based on gas detection, not visual observation; (4) responder injury pattern: deflagration (rapid pressure build then ignition) produces blast + thermal injury combination — extremity burns + tympanic rupture + secondary blunt trauma; (5) BESS sites should pre-load EmergencyNet GeoFence as ZONE_CHEM with HF + HCN + CO chemistry tags and 200m exclusion radius until cool-down confirmed.

Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE) of pressurized liquefied gas (LPG, propane, butane, ammonia, chlorine) is the highest-yield single-vessel industrial blast hazard. Reference incidents: 1984 Mexico City San Juanico LPG depot (~500 dead, 7,000+ injured), 2017 Tlahuelilpan Mexico fuel pipeline fire BLEVE (~135 dead). BLEVE physics: liquid in pressurized vessel is heated above its atmospheric boiling point; vessel structural failure releases liquid that flashes to vapor instantly, generating massive volume expansion + ignition + fireball + projectile vessel fragments. Operational lessons: (1) BLEVE-prone vessels (LPG bullets, rail cars, road tankers) require 'cooling water' to top of vessel during fire impingement to prevent metal weakening — fire crews directly applying water from overhead position; failure to cool top during 5-15 min fire exposure produces BLEVE; (2) exclusion zone: minimum 800m radius for road tanker, 1.6 km for rail tank car, until fire suppression confirmed and tank intact; (3) projectile fragments documented to 1 km+ (Mexico City 1984), with rocket-propelled tank shells — exclusion not just radial but accounting for tank orientation; (4) injury pattern in BLEVE: thermal (radiant + flame contact, 90%+ TBSA in close victims), primary blast lung, tympanic rupture, secondary blunt from projectiles, tertiary from blast wave throwing victims; modern doctrine: BLEVE survivors universally evaluated for blast lung, rhabdomyolysis, and CO/HCN inhalation. EmergencyNet ZONE_CHEM polygon with BLEVE-radius for LPG / NH3 storage facilities.

Anhydrous ammonia (NH3) release from industrial refrigeration (cold storage warehouses, food processing plants, ice rinks) is among the most common accidental large-volume toxic releases globally. Reference incidents: 2017 Fern Hill US (16 hospitalized), 2019 Beach Park IL (37 affected, 2 ICU). Operational features: (1) NH3 is highly water-soluble, irritant gas with strong odor at <5 ppm (well below toxic threshold of 25 ppm 8-hour TWA, 300 ppm IDLH); odor warning is generally protective unless concentration exceeds nasal saturation; (2) symptom progression: <50 ppm eye/throat irritation, 50-300 ppm severe mucosal irritation, >300 ppm pulmonary edema (delayed 6-24h), >1000 ppm laryngospasm + immediate fatality risk; (3) decontamination is water-based — copious water irrigation of skin/eyes for at least 15-20 minutes; clothing removal mandatory; do NOT neutralize with acidic solution (exothermic); (4) responder PPE: SCBA + Level A for entry to high-concentration zones (>300 ppm); Level B acceptable for downwind decon zone with continuous gas monitoring; (5) shelter-in-place vs evacuation: NH3 lighter than air, dissipates upward; downwind shelter-in-place with sealed windows generally safer than evacuation through plume for short releases <30 minutes; longer releases require evacuation; (6) ice rink incidents specifically: hockey players and spectators with cardiopulmonary disease at high risk from indoor accumulation. EmergencyNet ZONE_CHEM with NH3-tagged facilities should auto-flag downwind residential zones for shelter-in-place advisory.

Mass gatherings (Hajj, Olympics, music festivals, religious pilgrimages) carry elevated risk of communicable disease transmission and have produced specific MCI doctrine. Reference outbreaks: 2009 H1N1 at Hajj (returning pilgrims as vectors), 2014 MERS-CoV at Hajj (continuous monitoring program), 2020 Tablighi Jamaat super-spreader (early COVID-19 South / Southeast Asia transmission). Operational doctrine codified by WHO Mass Gatherings Health unit: (1) pre-event surveillance — case definition for high-risk pathogens 60 days pre-event with returning population follow-up 14 days post; (2) on-site temperature screening at venue entry plus respiratory symptom triage — yellow tags receive medical evaluation rather than entry; (3) on-site isolation tents with negative pressure for suspect cases pending evaluation; (4) host-country Ministry of Health coordination with origin-country health authorities — bidirectional alerting on identified cluster; (5) anti-microbial stockpile pre-position at major event venues — ciprofloxacin (meningococcal prophylaxis at Hajj is standard), oseltamivir (influenza), doxycycline (leptospirosis post-flooding); (6) digital health declarations + vaccination card verification became COVID-19 standard, persisting for international gatherings; (7) recurring lesson: mass gatherings concentrate vulnerability and connect previously-distant populations — outbreak amplifier function. Relevance: EmergencyNet anomaly_detector RESP_CLUSTER + GI_CLUSTER + febrile pattern at mass-gathering geo-fence triggers infectious disease workup, not just trauma escalation.

Oil refinery vapor cloud explosions (VCE) — typically light hydrocarbons (propane, butane, ethylene, gasoline vapor) — are among the highest-yield process industry incidents. Reference incidents: 2005 Texas City BP refinery (15 dead, 180+ injured, gasoline VCE during isomerization unit startup), 2009 Buncefield UK (43 injured, largest peacetime explosion in Europe, gasoline storage VCE), 2020 Beirut port (parallel mechanism scaled to ammonium nitrate). VCE physics: flammable vapor cloud at concentrations between LEL (lower explosive limit) and UEL (upper explosive limit), usually 1-10% in air, ignites and flame-front accelerates through cloud — confinement (between buildings, in vapor accumulation pockets) escalates to detonation with peak overpressures 50-500 kPa. Operational features: (1) responders must NOT approach vapor cloud — even cell phone or portable radio sparks are credible ignition sources; gas detection from upwind position dictates approach; (2) vapor migrates downhill (gases heavier than air) — sheltered pits, basements, drainage channels accumulate flammable concentrations long after release ceases; (3) injury pattern after VCE detonation: tertiary blast (thrown into structures), secondary penetrating glass / debris, primary blast lung in close victims, thermal burns (50-90% TBSA in immediate vicinity), CO/HCN inhalation; refinery fires often combine VCE + sustained pool fire requiring foam suppression; (4) EmergencyNet zone_type: ZONE_CHEM with refinery + flammability tags, with downwind exclusion 1-3 km depending on inventory.
